nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily shaded blackwater streams, forest pools, and peat swamps on Bangka Island, where water is stained dark brown by tannins from decomposing leaf litter and submerged vegetation. Hardness is near zero and conductivity extremely low.

decorPreferences: dense leaf litter (Indian almond/Catappa leaves) to lower pH and stain water
driftwood and bogwood to leach tannins
caves and hollow structures for spawning and retreat
fine-leaved or floating plants (Java moss, Salvinia, hornwort)
dim or heavily filtered lighting — shade with floating plants
dark substrate (fine black sand or peat)
peat filtration or blackwater extract to achieve pH 4.0–5.5

diet.notes: In the wild the Licorice Gourami feeds on tiny invertebrates, micro-crustaceans, and zooplankton. In captivity it requires live or frozen micro-foods — micro worms, Daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Cyclops, and Grindal worms. Dry foods are rarely accepted; some individuals can be conditioned onto frozen Cyclops or finely ground flake, but live food remains essential for long-term health and breeding condition. Feed small amounts twice daily.

goodWith: conspecifics (kept as pairs or small groups in well-planted tanks)
other Parosphromenus species in species-only setups
small peaceful blackwater fish of similar size (Boraras spp., Sundadanio spp.)
small Otocinclus in matched water chemistry
avoidWith: larger or boisterous fish (will be outcompeted or bullied)
hard-water community fish incompatible with pH 4–5.5
any fish requiring neutral to alkaline water

Wikimedia Commons category for P. deissneri; verify image quality and licensing on access.

summary: The Licorice Gourami (Parosphromenus deissneri) is a tiny, IUCN Critically Endangered blackwater specialist from Bangka Island, Indonesia, demanding pH 4.0–5.5, near-zero hardness, tannin-stained water, and live micro-foods. True P. deissneri is rarely available; most trade fish are other Parosphromenus species.
careNotes: The Licorice Gourami (Parosphromenus deissneri) is widely regarded as one of the most demanding freshwater fish in the hobby. It is a strict blackwater specialist native to shallow peat-swamp streams on Bangka Island, Indonesia, where water is extremely soft (near-zero general hardness and carbonate hardness), highly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), and darkly tannin-stained from decomposing vegetation. Replicating this chemistry is non-negotiable: fish kept above pH 5.5 or in water with any measurable hardness typically decline and die within months. Use RO or rainwater buffered only with peat, Indian almond (Catappa) leaves, and driftwood; tap water should not be used without full demineralisation in hard-water areas. Tanks should be heavily decorated with leaf litter, driftwood, caves, and dense fine-leaved plants with very dim lighting. A dedicated species tank or a carefully chosen blackwater nano community is strongly recommended — these fish are easily outcompeted for food and stressed by larger or boisterous tankmates. Feed exclusively on live or frozen micro-foods (micro worms, Daphnia, baby brine shrimp nauplii, Cyclops); dry foods are rarely accepted. A water temperature of 24–26°C is optimal; slow, gentle filtration through a sponge filter is preferred, as the species inhabits near-still waters and is sensitive to strong current. A critically important caveat: the vast majority of fish sold in the trade as ‘P. deissneri’ or ‘Licorice Gourami’ are actually other Parosphromenus species — most commonly P. parvulus, P. linkei, P. nagyi, or undescribed relatives. The genus contains more than 20 described species, and accurate identification requires close examination of fin patterning, coloration, and geographic provenance. Sourcing fish from specialist breeders participating in conservation programmes — such as those coordinated by the Parosphromenus Project (parosphromenus-project.org) — is strongly advised over purchasing from general trade. P. deissneri is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List, a status driven by peat-swamp habitat destruction on Bangka Island from palm oil agriculture and tin mining.
breedingNotes: The Licorice Gourami is a cave spawner. Males establish and defend a spawning cave — a coconut shell half, PVC tube, or similar hollow structure — and engage in intense colour displays during courtship, developing vivid blue-black banding with iridescent blue fin markings. After spawning, the male guards and fans the egg cluster. While some closely related Parosphromenus species are mouthbrooders, P. deissneri is primarily a cave and bubble-nest guarder. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or micro-worms as first foods before advancing to freshly hatched Artemia nauplii. Breeding success depends on maintaining pH below 5.5, near-zero hardness, and a reliable supply of live micro-foods. Given the species' dire conservation status and ongoing wild habitat loss, captive breeding is considered an important conservation act; surplus fry are best shared with other hobbyists through the Parosphromenus Project network.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Parosphromenus deissneri
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (pH 4.0–5.5, temperature 22–28°C, near-zero hardness), habitat description (Bangka Island blackwater peat swamps), diet (live micro-foods), care requirements, and breeding notes. Cross-referenced with FishBase and Parosphromenus Project for taxonomy, IUCN status, and trade misidentification notes. Recommend keeping both entries: fw-fish-395 as the species-level entry for P. deissneri sensu stricto; fw-fish-397 repurposed as a Parosphromenus spp. genus-group/care guide entry covering the broader 20+ species complex and trade misidentification. If merged, alsoKnownAs and careNotes of fw-fish-395 should absorb genus-level trade variation notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Parosphromenus deissneri
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (to ~3.5 cm), native range (Bangka Island, Sumatra region, Indonesia), and IUCN Critically Endangered status confirmed. Distribution map cross-checked against nativeRange entry.

additional.name: Parosphromenus Project
additional.notes: Specialist conservation-breeding organisation coordinating captive populations of all Parosphromenus species. Source for trade misidentification prevalence (most ‘P. deissneri’ in trade are P. parvulus, P. linkei, or other congeners), genus species count (20+ described species), IUCN status, and conservation breeding guidance. Key reference for hobbyistAdvisory and careNotes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Licorice Gourami care
additional.notes: UK hobbyist trade coverage of Parosphromenus spp.; water chemistry requirements (pH 4–5, very soft), feeding (live micro-foods required), tank setup, and specialist-only recommendation corroborated. Notes widespread misidentification of trade fish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Gourami Care Overview
additional.notes: General gourami care reference. No dedicated P. deissneri page; Aquarium Co-Op does not carry or document specialist blackwater gouramis at the species level. Cited for completeness per source priority list; specific P. deissneri parameters sourced from SeriouslyFish and Parosphromenus Project.

fish.breedingNotes: Males defend a hollow spawning site and fan the eggs after spawning. Breeding requires pH below 5.5, near-zero hardness, and an abundant supply of live micro-foods for both conditioning adults and feeding newly hatched fry. Fry are started on infusoria or micro-worms before being advanced to Artemia nauplii. The combination of strict water chemistry and micro-food requirements places this species in the advanced breeding category. Conservation breeding is strongly encouraged.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: A strict blackwater specialist requiring pH 4.0–5.5, tannin-stained water, and near-zero hardness — conditions that are difficult to maintain and incompatible with most community setups. Listed as Critically Endangered by the IUCN and endemic to a narrow range on Bangka Island. Fish sold under this name are frequently other Parosphromenus species; correct identification requires specialist knowledge. Best suited to experienced hobbyists engaged in conservation breeding.
